title: The protein stability curve
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: A protein is only marginally stable — a few tens of kJ/mol, the energy of a couple of hydrogen bonds. Plot its free energy of unfolding ΔG against temperature and you get the famous stability curve: a downward arch that is positive (folded) only over a window. Cross the top edge and it melts (heat denaturation, here Tm=60°C); cross the bottom edge and — counter-intuitively — it unfolds again in the cold. The fraction-folded curve is the sigmoid you measure in a CD or DSC melt. Marginal stability is why a few degrees of fever, or a point mutation, can tip a protein over the edge.
